Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time on weekends, but with limited resources at home and juggling extracurriculars, I often ended up cramming whenever I could find a quiet moment. I relied heavily on reading and writing notes by hand since that's how I learn best. Most weeks I'd manage about 8 hours total, but it was scattered across random pockets of time rather than any structured schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I barely touched anything, and then right before an exam or assignment, I'd go into intense study mode. Being 18 and dealing with financial stress at home meant I couldn't always prioritize studying the way I wanted to. The anxiety of not having a consistent routine actually made things harder.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or a notification from a classmate in our peer support group that would push me to log in. Sometimes I'd check just to see if there were any updates or new materials posted. The peer interactions were actually a big motivator—knowing my study group was active made me feel like I needed to keep up even when my motivation was low.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became more routine, especially when our peer support group was really active in discussions. We'd kind of hold each other accountable, so I'd check in to see what others were posting. But honestly, it never became a deeply ingrained habit for me because my attendance and engagement were so inconsistent overall. The routine only stuck when there was social pressure from peers.
Q5: The discussion forums and reading materials were most useful for me since I'm a read/write learner. I could download the readings, annotate them, and then come back to discuss with peers. The peer interactions—all 22 of them—really helped solidify concepts because explaining things to others or hearing their perspectives made the material click. Videos were okay but I preferred text-based resources I could highlight and review.
Q6: I definitely didn't complete a lot of the tasks—my completion rate was pretty low. I'd often skip optional quizzes or supplementary videos because I was already overwhelmed and had to prioritize what would directly impact my grade. Some resources just felt redundant when I'd already understood the concept from the readings. With limited time and high anxiety, I had to be strategic about what I engaged with.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or assignments, or if it helped me understand something I was struggling with. I also valued anything that involved peer interaction because that kept me accountable and less isolated. If something felt like busy work or wasn't clearly connected to assessment, I'd probably skip it given how stretched thin I already was.
Q8: Required activities carried this weight of obligation that honestly increased my anxiety, but I'd force myself through them. Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford time-wise, even if they might have been helpful. There was almost a guilt with optional stuff—like I knew I should do it, but the stress of everything else made me deprioritize anything that wasn't mandatory. Required work got done eventually; optional work rarely did.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download readings and PDFs, print them when I could afford to at the library, and study from my handwritten notes. A lot of my learning happened through re-reading my notes and rewriting key concepts—that's just how my brain works. The LMS was more of a content delivery system than where my real learning took place.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or working through problems on paper that would never show up in any log. I'd also study with peers in person, quizzing each other or discussing concepts, which was incredibly valuable but invisible to the system. My exam score of 96.74 reflects learning that happened largely outside the digital platform.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading or lecture material once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never return to the LMS version again. I preferred having physical or saved copies I could access without internet since my resources are limited and connectivity isn't always reliable. One download could turn into hours of offline study that the system would never capture.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engagement became really hard, especially when my anxiety spiked or when financial stress at home was particularly intense. Sometimes I'd go days without logging in because I just couldn't face it emotionally. My resilience isn't great, so when things piled up, I'd kind of shut down rather than push through. Those were the times my attendance and task completion really suffered.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. The workload from five online courses was overwhelming, and fatigue from anxiety made it hard to concentrate. When I was confused about something and didn't have the energy to seek help, I'd just disengage temporarily. Competing responsibilities at home, given our financial situation, meant studying often took a backseat to more immediate concerns.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have videos playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. The anxiety would make it hard to focus even when I was technically 'present' on the platform. I'd sometimes spend time on the LMS just to feel like I was doing something productive, but nothing was actually sinking in. Those sessions felt empty and frustrating.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even important ones. The anxiety made starting tasks feel insurmountable sometimes—like this paralysis where I knew I needed to do something but couldn't make myself begin. My assignment completion rate shows that clearly. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I felt more capable, but 'later' often became 'too late' or 'barely on time.'
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that reliably got me to engage. I'd have these bursts of intense activity right before something was due, which is probably why my login frequency and response times look the way they do. Without a deadline, I'd drift and procrastinate. The pressure was stressful but also necessary for me to actually produce work.
Q17: Feedback definitely motivated me when it was positive—seeing that my work was good quality encouraged me to keep going. But if feedback was delayed or critical, it could spiral my anxiety and make me avoid the course for a while. The peer support helped buffer this because my study group would help me process feedback and stay on track even when I felt discouraged.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with five courses running simultaneously. I appreciated having materials available online, but the sheer volume made it hard to stay on top of everything. The discussion components were good because they connected me with peers, but the flexibility also meant I could easily fall behind without immediate consequences. More structured check-ins might have helped me stay consistent.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement easier since I could study offline. The peer interaction features were also helpful for someone like me who benefits from social accountability. What made it harder was the lack of clear prioritization—everything felt equally important, so I couldn't always tell what to focus on when time was limited. More guidance on essential vs. supplementary content would have reduced my anxiety.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or overwhelmed and keeps checking the LMS hoping to feel prepared, but can't actually bring themselves to complete the work. It's like the activity becomes a form of procrastination or reassurance-seeking rather than productive engagement. The gap between browsing and submitting might reflect emotional barriers rather than lack of effort.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience pretty closely. My LMS activity was moderate at best, but I scored really well on exams because my real learning happened offline through reading and writing notes. The system can't see the hours I spent with printed materials or studying with peers in person. Low digital activity doesn't necessarily mean low learning—it might just mean different learning strategies.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and how often they logged in, which indicates some level of engagement. But they completely miss offline study, the quality of attention during online sessions, emotional state, and learning that happens through peer conversations outside the platform. They capture behavior but not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's showing up mentally even when it's hard, asking questions when confused, and connecting with peers to deepen learning. For me, engagement also means managing my anxiety enough to actually absorb what I'm studying rather than just going through the motions. It's about genuine investment in learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group meant to my success, or the hours of offline studying that led to my exam performance. It wouldn't capture my anxiety levels or how hard I had to fight just to engage at all some days. It also can't show that despite low attendance and completion rates, I genuinely understood and retained the material well enough to earn a distinction. The numbers tell one story; my actual learning journey is much more complex.
